ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Chilli Gobi (deep-fried battered cauliflower in Indo-Chinese sauce). Mapped to cauliflower food product as the closest defensible FoodOn anchor; preparation method and sauce components are not captured by this code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (cauliflower, cooked) is the closest defensible match. The deep-fried battered preparation and Indo-Chinese sauce profile are not represented in FoodEx2 at this level of specificity.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Chilli Gobi or equivalent Indo-Chinese cauliflower dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Chilli Gobi is approximately 300 grams, providing roughly 550 kcal, suitable as a shared starter or a light individual main course.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; USDA FoodData Central — Cauliflower, cooked, boiled, drained (SR Legacy #11136); HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India; USDA Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source contains a direct entry for Chilli Gobi as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from: (1) ICMR-NIN data for cauliflower and Indian vegetable preparations; (2) USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for cauliflower cooked and cornflour; (3) HPB Singapore database for comparable Indo-Chinese fried vegetable dishes. The input per_100g values (energy 183 kcal, protein 4.0 g, fat 11.67 g, carbohydrate 16.67 g) are consistent with this composite estimation and are retained with medium confidence. Trans fat 0.17 g/100g is plausible from deep-frying oil. Sodium 400 mg/100g is consistent with soy sauce and chilli sauce contributions.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; analogous estimate based on battered deep-fried vegetable with refined flour coating and sweet-savoury sauce
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chilli Gobi in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on analogous foods: deep-fried battered vegetables coated in refined wheat/cornflour batter (GI ~65–70 range per Sydney GI Database entries for similar battered fried foods) combined with a sweet-savoury sauce containing sugar and ketchup. The cornflour and maida batter are the dominant GI drivers. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × available carbohydrate per serving (25.0 g for 300 g portion) / 100 = 16.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg per 100 g, driven by soy sauce, chilli sauce, and tomato ketchup
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Chilli Gobi due to its sodium content of 400 mg/100 g (1200 mg per standard 300 g plate), which approaches 52% of the 2300 mg daily sodium limit in a single serving; request reduced-sauce preparation or substitute low-sodium soy sauce to bring sodium below 300 mg/100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate batter (maida/cornflour) and added sugar from sweet sauce yield a medium GI of 65 and glycemic load of 16.3 per 300 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Chilli Gobi with caution: the deep-fried refined flour batter and sweet chilli-ketchup sauce contribute 16.67 g carbohydrate and 5 g sugar per 100 g with a medium GI of approximately 65, resulting in a glycemic load of 16.3 per plate; request air-fried or baked preparation and reduced sauce to lower the glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation contributes 11.67 g total fat and 2.0 g saturated fat per 100 g, with 0.17 g trans fat from frying oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Chilli Gobi due to its deep-fried fat content (11.67 g fat, 2.0 g saturated fat, 0.17 g trans fat per 100 g) and high sodium (400 mg/100 g); the cauliflower and bell pepper base provides vitamin C (26.67 mg/100 g) and dietary fibre (2.67 g/100 g) as partial offsets, but the frying method and sauce sodium remain cardiovascular risk factors.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Chilli Gobi in its standard deep-fried form due to AGE formation from high-temperature frying; request air-fried or baked preparation to preserve cauliflower's sulforaphane anti-inflammatory benefit while eliminating AGE-related inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chilli Gobi is a popular Indo-Chinese vegetarian dish consisting of cauliflower florets coated in a spiced batter of cornflour and all-purpose flour (maida), deep-fried until crispy, then stir-fried with bell peppers, onions, ginger, garlic, and green chillies in a spicy, tangy, and slightly sweet sauce made from soy sauce, chilli sauce, and tomato ketchup. The dish originates from the Indo-Chinese culinary tradition and is widely served in Indian restaurants and food courts across Singapore as a starter or side dish. The texture contrasts crispy fried cauliflower with a sticky, glossy sauce coating. Nutritionally, it provides 183 kcal per 100 g with 11.67 g fat (including 2.0 g saturated fat and 0.17 g trans fat from deep-frying), 16.67 g carbohydrate (5 g sugar), 4.0 g protein, and 2.67 g dietary fibre. Sodium is high at 400 mg/100 g due to the sauce components. Vitamin C is notable at 26.67 mg/100 g from cauliflower and bell peppers. The dish is suitable for vegetarians, vegans, and halal diets but is not gluten-free due to wheat flour in the batter. Clinically, the high sodium content is a concern for hypertension, the refined carbohydrate batter and added sugar raise glycemic concerns for Type 2 Diabetes management, and the deep-fried preparation with trans fat is a cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis risk factor. A standard plate serving is approximately 300 g, providing around 550 kcal.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Chilli Gobi?
Is Chilli Gobi suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No single approved source contains a direct entry for Chilli Gobi as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN, USDA FoodData Central, and HPB Singapore data for cauliflower preparations and comparable Indo-Chinese fried vegetable dishes. The input values from the GrabFood-sourced record are consistent with this composite estimation and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework applied to battered deep-fried vegetables with refined flour coating.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Indo-Chinese fried cauliflower dish is not available in ICMR-NIN, USDA FoodData Central, or HPB Singapore databases as of 2026. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this specific composite preparation is not reported in the consulted approved sources, though cauliflower and bell peppers are known to contribute approximately 200–300 mg/100 g in raw form. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific composite dish preparation are not available in the approved sources consulted. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed as negligible/zero for this fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for cauliflower and vegetable oil preparations. 5. Two portions were listed in the input (350 g and 300 g both marked is_default=true); 300 g is adopted as the default serving size as the more conservative and commonly cited restaurant portion for a shared starter plate. 6. The input food name contained emoji characters (❤️💋); these have been removed for the canonical food_name field. 7. Trans fat 0.17 g/100 g is plausible from repeated use of frying oil in commercial preparation; this value is retained from the input with medium confidence.
